text: The surgeon encounters a challenging case during laparoscopic cholecystectomy, with the liver visibly adherent to the anterior abdominal wall, restricting free retraction of the gallbladder towards the right shoulder.

Using a 30-degree telescope, the surgeon begins dissection above the rubial sulcus, peeling off the peritoneal reflection over the calyx region. The gallbladder is pulled laterally and caudally to enhance visibility of the medial reflection. Adhesions are addressed before peeling off the lateral peritoneal reflection, with the gallbladder pulled medially under traction.

The surgeon uses left-hand forceps to dissect the peritoneum laterally, ensuring to stay above the rubial sulcus. Approximately one-third of the peritoneal reflection over the gallbladder is dissected before addressing the Calot's region. Small bands or peritoneal reflections around the cystic duct are managed with a hook instrument.

The surgeon creates a window between the cystic duct and cystic artery, identifying the cystic artery for skeletonization. Using blunt dissection, the surgeon clears the cystic artery, making it visible. The gallbladder body is then dissected from the liver bed to clear the Calot's triangle, which is bounded laterally by the cystic duct, medially by the liver's undersurface, and inferiorly by the common hepatic duct. The triangle contains the cystic artery and a lymph node.

The surgeon applies three clips on the cystic duct: one distal near the neck of the gallbladder and two proximal, ensuring a minimum distance of two millimeters between the proximal clips. A clip is also placed on the cystic artery. The cystic duct is cut between the clips using scissors, and the cystic artery is coagulated with a hook. The gallbladder is then separated from the liver bed using electrocautery.

The surgeon uses an L hook to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed, employing electrocautery to cook the tissue. The heel of the hook is used when the dissection plane is difficult to find. As the dissection progresses towards the fundus of the gallbladder, forceps are readjusted to hold the gallbladder near its body, facilitating the dissection in the fundus region.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree telescope to enhance visibility in cases where the liver adheres to the anterior abdominal wall. Electrocautery with a hook and blunt dissection using a Maryland are employed, ensuring dissection remains above the rooier sulcus for safety.
